Hepatitis B | Public Health | County of Santa Clara

Hepatitis B
Tiếng Việt
Protect yourself and your children from hepatitis B.
Infants are recommended to receive the first hepatitis B vaccine dose at birth, followed by two additional doses.
Adults age 18 and older should talk to their doctor about getting tested for hepatitis B and getting vaccinated if they are not already vaccinated.
Hepatitis B Prevention Program
The Public Health Department’s Hepatitis B Prevention Program works to address the burden of disease from hepatitis B on Santa Clara County residents.
What is hepatitis B?
Hepatitis B is a serious disease caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV). Hepatitis B is vaccine-preventable disease infection that damages the liver.
The type of hepatitis B you have depends on how long you have had the virus in your body, the health of your liver, and other factors. A blood test will indicate which one you have.
Acute hepatitis B
Acute hepatitis B is a short-term illness that occurs within the first 6 months after exposure to HBV. Some people with acute hepatitis B have no symptoms or only a mild illness. For others, acute hepatitis B can cause a more severe illness that requires hospitalization.
Chronic hepatitis B
Acute hepatitis B can lead to a lifelong infection known as chronic hepatitis B. Left untreated, chronic hepatitis B can cause serious health problems, including liver damage, cirrhosis, liver cancer, and even death.
How can it spread?
Birth:
Hepatitis B is spread from infected pregnant mother to child at birth
Body fluids:
Hepatitis B is spread when blood, semen, or other body fluids from a person infected with the hepatitis B virus enters someone who is not infected
Direct contact with the blood or open sores of a person who has hepatitis B
Sharing equipment that has been contaminated with blood such as needles, syringes, and even medical equipment such as glucose monitors
Sharing razors or toothbrushes
Sex:
Hepatitis B can be spread through unprotected sexual contact with an infected person
Hepatitis B is NOT spread through sneezing, coughing, hugging, breastfeeding, or through food or water.
Learn more about risk factors for hepatitis B
What are the symptoms of hepatitis B?
Many people with hepatitis B do not have symptoms and do not know they are infected. If symptoms occur from an acute infection, they can appear up to 90 days after exposure. Most people with chronic infection do not have symptoms until much later in life, often decades after exposure. Symptoms of hepatitis B can include:
Fever
Fatigue
Loss of appetite
Nausea
Vomiting
Abdominal pain
Dark urine
Light-colored stools
Joint pain
Jaundice
Why is testing for hepatitis B important?
Many people with hepatitis B do not look or feel sick, so testing is the only way to know if they are infected.
About 1 in 2 people living with hepatitis B
do not know
they are infected. Testing plays an important role in the detection, classification, and management of hepatitis B, contributing to preventing liver damage.
Talk to your doctor today to get tested for hepatitis B.
Who should get tested for hepatitis B?
All adults 18 years and older should get tested for hepatitis B at least once in their lifetime, even if they are vaccinated. People at
higher risk for hepatitis B
should be tested more frequently, including:
All pregnant
people
during each pregnancy, preferably during the first trimester
Infants born to pregnant people with hepatitis B virus infection
People with ongoing risk for exposure, including people who live with someone who has hepatitis B or health care workers who are exposed to blood on the job
People born in Asia, Africa, and other regions with moderate or high rates of hepatitis B
Unvaccinated people in the U.S. whose parents are from regions with high rates of hepatitis B
Anyone having sex with a person infected with hepatitis B
Men who have sex with men
People who inject drugs or share needles
People with HIV infection
People who are on dialysis
People who have liver damage or inflammation
Take a risk assessment
You can take a short
risk assessment
to determine your risk for hepatitis B. The only way to diagnose hepatitis B is by a blood test.
Who should get vaccinated?
The best way to prevent hepatitis B is to get vaccinated. The hepatitis B vaccine is a series of two or three shots depending on the vaccine brand. The hepatitis B vaccine is safe and effective. It gives you long-term protection against illness from acute and chronic infection.
The hepatitis B vaccine is recommended* for:
All infants and children (at least 3 doses starting at birth)
All children and adolescents under 19 years of age who have not been vaccinated
Any child or adult under 60 years of age who is unvaccinated or doesn't know their vaccination history
Some people 60 years and older:
Unvaccinated people 60 years and older with
risk factors
for hepatitis B should get vaccinated
Unvaccinated people 60 years and older without known risk factors for hepatitis B may be vaccinated
More information on where to get vaccinated
*The Public Health Department aligns with the California Department of Public Health (CDPH)
immunization guidance
, which is based on recommendations from the
American Academy of Pediatrics
for children and the
American Academy of Family Physicians
for adults.
